1: The third Levitical census (ch1 counted Israel for war, ch3 counted all Levites for their replacement role, ch4 counts the service-age Levites for active tabernacle transport). The double address to Moses and Aaron signals that this census involves priestly supervision — Aaron's sons oversee the actual covering of the holy objects before the Levites may carry them.
2: The Kohathites are counted first because they carry the most holy objects. The census is organized leḇêt ʾăḇōtām — by ancestral houses — within the broader clan structure, reflecting the patrilineal organization of Israelite society that also determines inheritance, land allocation, and levitical assignments throughout Numbers.
3: The age bracket 30–50 (mibbɛn šəlōšîm šānâ wamaʿəlâ wəʿad ben-ḥămiššîm šānâ) defines the period of full active service. Later in 8:24-25 the age of entry is set at 25 for preliminary service, and at 50 one retires from transport service. The 30-year minimum aligns with when a priest could begin full ministry in later tradition (Luke 3:23 notes Jesus began ministry at approximately this age, echoing the Levitical-priestly pattern).
4: The Kohathites are assigned the most holy objects (qōḏeš haqqǒḏāšîm). In the P tradition's holiness gradient, the holy of holies is the most sacred space and its contents the most sacred objects. Kohathite privilege — carrying the ark, table, lampstand, altars — is matched by extraordinary danger: they must not touch or see the holy things (vv15,20) on pain of death.
5: The covering procedure begins with Aaron and his sons (the priests): they must wrap everything before the Kohathites arrive. The inner veil (pārōket hamāsāk) — the curtain separating the holy of holies from the holy place — comes down first and wraps the ark of the covenant. The ark cannot be approached by Kohathites until it is already covered by the priests; even the high privilege of transport comes with this mediating barrier.
6: Three layers protect the ark: the inner veil (v5), a fine leather covering (ʿôr tāḥaš — perhaps dugong or porpoise hide, a durable water-resistant material), and a blue cloth (bəgeḏ kəlîl təkēlet) on the outside. The visible outer cloth is blue — the color associated with the sky and with priestly/royal dignity throughout the tabernacle (Exod 25:4; 28:5). The ark presents as a blue-wrapped object to the Kohathite carriers, with no gold or sacred form visible.
7: The table of the Presence (šulḥan happānîm) — the showbread table — is covered in blue cloth with its dishes, plates, bowls, and cups left on it. The table's sacred service items remain in place during transport, maintained in their cultic arrangement even while in transit. This preserves the ritual order within the movement of the whole sanctuary.
8: The table covering adds a scarlet cloth over the blue — the only object with this double-cloth arrangement in ch4. Red/scarlet (šānî tôlaʿat) was used in purification rites (Lev 14; Num 19) and in tabernacle construction (Exod 25:4). The leather covering goes over the scarlet. The table thus has the most elaborate wrapping: blue (inner), scarlet (middle), leather (outer).
9: The lampstand (mənorat hammāʾôr) and all its accessories — snuffers, trays, oil vessels — are wrapped in blue cloth. The lampstand is the only object in the tabernacle with continuous function (it burns perpetually, Exod 27:20-21); its transport covering puts its fire out, with all implements gathered for protection. The lampstand's transport symbolizes the temporary suspension of the divine light during movement.
10: The lampstand assembly is placed on a carrying frame (môṭ) within the leather covering. The môṭ — carrying pole/frame — appears for the lampstand and for the table (v8) as distinct from the carrying poles (baddîm) used for the ark and altars. The distinction reflects different modes of transport for different categories of sacred furniture.
11: The golden altar of incense (mizbaḥ hazzāhāḇ) is covered in blue cloth and leather — the same covering combination as the ark (vv5-6). The incense altar's blue outer covering gives it visual parity with the ark during transport, signaling its high status among the holy objects. The incense altar stood directly before the veil in the holy place, making it the closest non-ark object to the divine presence.
12: All the utensils of ministry (kəlê haššərēt) used in the sanctuary are gathered into a blue cloth, covered in leather, and placed on the carrying frame. The comprehensive gathering ensures no sacred vessel is left behind or transported separately outside the covered Kohathite loads.
13: The bronze altar of burnt offering (mizbaḥ hannəḥōšet) presents a unique challenge: it is full of ashes from the daily burnt offerings. The ashes must be removed (ûdišnû ʾet-hammizbēaḥ) and a purple cloth spread over it. Purple (ʾargāmān) — a dye from Murex sea snails, extremely expensive in antiquity — covers the bronze altar, whose status as the most used sacrificial object earns it distinct treatment.
14: All the bronze altar's utensils — fire pans (maḥtōt), forks (mizlāgōt), shovels (yaʿîm), basins (mizrāqōt), the grating — are placed on the purple-covered altar before the leather covering goes over everything. The implements of blood and fire are treated with the same ritual care as the objects of incense and bread in the holy place.
15: The critical verse: only after Aaron and his sons finish covering everything may the Kohathites come and lift (ûnəśāʾû hakkəhātîm). But they must not touch (wəlōʾ-yiggəʿû ʾel-haqqōḏeš) the holy things; death follows from touching. The word wāmētû — and they shall die — is the same death-warning used for unauthorized priestly and lay approach throughout Leviticus and Numbers. The privilege of proximity is precisely what makes the danger real.
16: Eleazar son of Aaron has oversight of the entire service: the oil of the light, the fragrant incense, the regular grain offering, and the anointing oil. These are the items requiring continuous provision and priestly management. Eleazar's role as administrator over the whole tabernacle service anticipates his later position as chief priest after Aaron's death (Num 20:25-28).
17: A fresh divine speech addresses a specific danger unique to the Kohathites. The warning is directed at Moses and Aaron — the leaders must take action to protect the Kohathite clan from being cut off (yikkārēt šēḇeṭ mišpəḥōt haqqəhātî).
18: ʾal-tašmîdû ʾet-šēḇeṭ mišpəḥōt haqqəhātî mittôk halwiyyim — do not let the Kohathite clan be destroyed from among the Levites. The warning addresses the structural irony: those most privileged in the Levitical hierarchy (Kohath is the clan of Moses and Aaron, Exod 6:18-20) face the greatest risk of death from their very assignment. Special privilege and special danger coincide throughout the P legislation.
19: The protective procedure: Aaron and his sons shall assign each man to his specific task and his specific load. The individualized assignment prevents any Kohathite from being in the wrong place at the wrong time relative to the uncovered holy objects. The protective procedure is not ritual but organizational: careful priestly administration is what keeps the Kohathites alive.
20: wəlōʾ-yāḇōʾû lirʾôt kəballēaʿ ʾet-haqqōḏeš wāmētû — they must not go in to look at the holy things even for a moment, or they will die. The word kəballēaʿ — even for a moment/as in swallowing — suggests the briefest visual contact with the unwrapped holy objects is fatal. This is not mere metaphor: Uzzah's death when touching the ark (2 Sam 6:6-7) and the death of the Bethshemeshites who looked into the ark (1 Sam 6:19) narrate exactly this principle as historical reality.
21: The Gershonite census commands follow the Kohathite section. The three-part structure of ch4 — Kohath, Gershon, Merari — mirrors the clan hierarchy established in ch3, where Kohath's position in the middle physically (the most central location around the tabernacle) reflects its cultic centrality.
22: The Gershonites are counted by the same method: ancestral houses, clans. The administrative uniformity across the three Levitical censuses reflects the P tradition's systematic approach to organization — each clan is processed through the same bureaucratic procedure even though their assignments differ considerably.
23: The same 30–50 age bracket applies to the Gershonites. The phrase kol-bāʾ liṣbōʾ ṣāḇāʾ laʿăḇōḏ ʿăḇōḏâ — all who qualify to serve, to do work — uses the military conscription vocabulary (ṣāḇāʾ = host/army) for the Levitical service. The Levites' tabernacle work is framed as a form of military service, with the same age requirement and organizational structure.
24: The Gershonite assignment: ʿăḇōḏâ ûmassāʾ — service and carrying. The two-term description distinguishes service (cultic duties) from carrying (physical transport), though for the Gershonites these are unified: their service is defined by carrying the fabric components of the tabernacle.
25: The Gershonite load: the tabernacle's curtains (yərîʿōt hammiškān), the tent covering (ʾōhel môʿēd), the leather outer covering, the screen (māsāk) for the tent entrance. These are the fabric elements that defined the space of the sanctuary; the Kohathites carry the holy furniture inside the space, while the Gershonites carry the space itself.
26: The courtyard hangings (qalʿê heḥāṣēr), the screen at the gate, and all the cords (kol-yōtēdōt) are part of the Gershonite load. Everything made of woven or natural fiber — linen, wool, goat hair — belongs to Gershon. The Gershonites' work is lighter in symbolic terms (they carry the outer rather than the inner) but enormous in physical scale: the combined curtain surface of the tabernacle was massive.
27: All Gershonite work — in carrying and in all service (kol-ʿăḇōḏōtām ûlkol-massāʾām) — is done under Aaron and his sons' direction. The Gershonites have no independent authority; they receive their assignments from Ithamar son of Aaron (v28). The Levitical clans function as organized labor under priestly supervision throughout the P system.
28: Ithamar son of Aaron oversees the Gershonites. The assignment of Eleazar to the Kohathites (v16) and Ithamar to the Gershonites and Merarites (v33) reflects the division of priestly supervisory responsibility between Aaron's two remaining sons after Nadab and Abihu died (Lev 10:1-2). The administrative structure accommodates the reduced priestly family.
29: The Merarite census follows the same formula as the other two clans. The Merarites were historically the largest of the three Levitical clans (ch3: 6,200 vs. Kohath's 8,600 and Gershon's 7,500 — Merarites were second largest by total count), and the active-service census confirms their substantial numbers (v44: 3,200).
30: Same age bracket (30–50) for the Merarites. The uniformity of age requirements across all three clans establishes that mature physical capacity (both for the heavy work and for the responsibility) is the baseline requirement for tabernacle transport service regardless of the specific assignment.
31: The Merarite load: the structural hardware of the tabernacle — frames (qərāšîm), bars (bərîḥîm), pillars (ʿammûḏîm), and bases (ʾăḏānîm). While the Kohathites carried the most sacred content and the Gershonites carried the fabric envelope, the Merarites carried the skeleton — the heavy wood and metal framework that gave the tabernacle its structural integrity. Their load was probably the heaviest physically.
32: The surrounding courtyard pillars, their bases, pegs (yōtēḏōt), and ropes (mêtārîm) — all the hardware for the exterior — belong to Merarites. The detailed list of articles, with each man assigned to specific items by name, reflects the P tradition's careful inventory control. Specific assignment prevents any equipment from being lost in transit or claimed by the wrong carrier.
33: The Merarite work falls under Ithamar's supervision (parallel to the Gershonites, v28). The two less-holy clans are both supervised by the younger surviving Aaronic son. This administrative arrangement proved practically significant: Ithamar's line eventually housed the Eli priesthood (1 Sam 2:27-36) at Shiloh and beyond.
34: The census execution begins: Moses, Aaron, and the leaders count the Kohathites. The communal leadership (the same tribal leaders who administered the ch1 census) participates again here, making the Levitical service-census a matter of public record and communal accountability, not merely internal Levitical administration.
35: The count is defined with precision: age 30-50, all who qualified for service in the tent of meeting. The census criteria are explicit in the repetition — the count will be precise and limited to exactly those who meet the service-age requirement.
36: Kohathite service-age count: 2,750. The ch3 Kohathite total was 8,600 (all males, one month and up). The service-age cohort (30-50) is 2,750 — approximately 32% of the total — consistent with the service-age fraction one would expect in a living population given ancient mortality patterns.
37: The closing summary formula: counted by Moses and Aaron at YHWH's command through Moses. Each clan census closes with the same attribution — divine command through Moses, executed by Moses and Aaron. The census data is thereby classified as covenant compliance rather than administrative routine.
38: The Gershonite census results follow the same structure. The census of Gershon (vv38-41) mirrors Kohath exactly: the same formula, the same age bracket, the same attribution to divine command.
39: The Gershonite service-age count begins with the same qualifier: all from 30-50 who qualified to work in the tent of meeting. The uniform criteria applied across all three clans ensures that the combined total (v46-48) represents a consistent category.
40: Gershonite service-age count: 2,630. Slightly smaller than the Kohathite count despite Gershon being slightly larger overall — the ratio of service-age males to total males was apparently lower, reflecting natural demographic variation between the clans.
41: Same attribution formula: counted by Moses and Aaron at YHWH's command. The repetition of the formula three times — once per clan — emphasizes that each clan's census was a discrete act of covenantal compliance, not a single procedure applied uniformly.
42: The Merarite census follows. Despite being the structurally heaviest-load clan, the Merarites receive the same administrative treatment as the other clans. The P tradition's egalitarian procedural application — same formulas, same criteria, same attribution — reflects an ideology of uniform covenantal obligation across all Levitical clans.
43: Same age bracket, same qualification criteria. The Merarites who qualify for active transport service will be individually assigned their specific loads (v32) — the most granular assignment in all of ch4.
44: Merarite service-age count: 3,200. This is the largest service-age count of the three clans (Kohath: 2,750; Gershon: 2,630; Merari: 3,200), a reversal from the ch3 total-count ranking (Kohath was largest overall). The Merarites' service-age proportion was apparently highest — possibly because their heavy physical work required the prime-working-age cohort more fully than the other assignments.
45: The Merarite census attribution mirrors the previous two clans exactly. The three closing formulas (vv37,41,45) form a structural parallel, signaling the completion of the three-clan census and preparing for the combined summary that follows.
46: The combined summary: all Levites enrolled by Moses, Aaron, and the Israelite leaders. This combined accounting (kol-hapqûḏîm ʾăšer pāqaḏ Moshe wəʾahărōn wənəśîʾê yiśrāʾēl) reflects the tripartite leadership — prophet, priest, and tribal heads — who administered the census jointly. The total represents the entire trained service-force for the tabernacle at this point in the wilderness period.
47: The qualification criterion for the grand total: 30-50, qualified to perform both the service work (ʿăḇōḏat hāʿăḇōḏâ) and the carrying work (ʿăḇōḏat hammassāʾ). The doubled phrase with ʿăḇōḏâ — service — emphasizes the integration of cultic duty and physical labor in the Levitical assignment; these are not separable functions.
48: Combined service-age count: 8,580 (2,750 + 2,630 + 3,200 = 8,580). The census demonstrates the scale of the tabernacle's operational personnel: nearly nine thousand trained Levites in the prime service years, organized into three clans with distinct assignments, under priestly supervision. This is the standing infrastructure of Israel's covenant worship for the forty-year wilderness period.
49: The concluding formula: each was enrolled at YHWH's command through Moses, each assigned to his service and his load. The phrase ʾîš ʾîš ʿal-ʿăḇōḏātô wəʿal-massāʾô — each man to his service and to his load — emphasizes individual assignment within the communal system. The wilderness tabernacle operated through specific, named persons with specific, defined responsibilities — a model of ordered covenantal service.
